text: Hello everybody. This is edited video of robotic look anterior resection done at Astro Hospital, Bangalore. The patient here is a forty nine year old lady who was presented with bleeding PR since four months. Imaging revealed a proximal rectal growth with perirectal fat standing and few mesorectal nodes. After multidisciplinary tumor board meeting, she was planned for neoadjuvant chemoradiation.

Following neoadjuvant chemoradiation, the surgeon initiates dissection around the sacral promontory using a robotic system. The sigmoid mesocolon is lifted anteriorly and superiorly, developing a plane between the sigmoid mesocolon and the retroperitoneum, specifically between Toldt's fascia and the retroperitoneal fascia. The dissection proceeds cranially towards the origin of the inferior mesenteric artery and laterally towards Toldt's line, employing a medial to lateral approach. Soft tissue around the inferior mesenteric artery is dissected, with the artery and Toldt's fascia lifted anteriorly. The dissection continues inferiorly to the sacral promontory and laterally towards the lateral abdominal wall, with the ureter and gonadal pedicle visible falling towards the retroperitoneum. The inferior mesenteric artery is exposed and divided between Hem-o-lok clips.

The surgeon dissects soft tissue lateral and cranial to the inferior mesenteric artery, revealing the inferior mesenteric vein, which is then bared and divided between Hem-o-lok clips. The dissection continues in an avascular plane between the mesorectal fascia and pelvic fascia, preserving the nerves supplying the urinary bladder.

The iliac vessels and ureter are visible as the surgeon takes several pelvic cuts and begins dissection in the avascular plane between the mesorectal fascia and pelvic fascia. A fenestrated bipolar instrument is used to retract the rectum anteriorly and superiorly, while preserving the nerve bundles supplying the urinary bladder.

The surgeon continues the posterior dissection until the levator ani muscles are visible. The mesorectum is dissected bilaterally, maintaining the same plane. The lateral part of the mesorectum is further dissected, with much of the work done via the posterior route. The cranial part near the splenic flexure is mobilized.

The surgeon completes lateral peritoneal cuts from the left lateral aspect, reaching the posterior dissection plane. Further lateral resection on the left side is completed. Anteriorly, Douglas peritoneum cuts are made between the rectum and vagina to access the plane between them, with similar dissection performed on the right side. The assistant retracts the uterus during the procedure.

The surgeon performs mesorectal division on the left lateral side, facilitating tension-free anastomosis. The mesorectal cuts are made circumferentially, extending towards the left half of the mesorectum. A 12 mm assistant port in the suprapubic region is used for stapler placement, and an Acelon-M-M stapler is fired to divide the rectum, completing the circumferential dissection.

The surgeon marks the proximal site of division on the colon, approximately fifteen centimeters from the tumor. The colon is lifted anteriorly and inferiorly, and the mesocolon is divided cranial to the inferior mesenteric vein pedicle. A four-centimeter incision is made, and the colon is exteriorized. The proximal cut is completed, and an anastomosis is performed using a 29 mm circular stapler. An ileostomy is fashioned.
